sourceMovie.title: Troy
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Troy works as a war movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The war and action blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Kingdom of Heaven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Kingdom of Heaven (2005) captures the war appeal of Troy without simply imitating it. The action streak running through both films is what puts Kingdom of Heaven at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.
The war appeal of Troy — that tense edge, the action — translates cleanly into 300 (2007). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Spartacus
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Spartacus (1960) clearly understood what makes war work — the same quality that keeps Troy rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Gladiator
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Gladiator (2000) is the kind of film Troy fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The action overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Pirates of the Caribbean: At World's End shares Troy's gift for action — the 2007 film delivers adventure without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Spider-Man 3
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Troy compelling — the action, the adventure — shows up in Spider-Man 3 (2007) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Kill Bill: Vol. 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The action energy of Troy is alive in Kill Bill: Vol. 2 (2004), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Star Wars: Episode III - Revenge of the Sith
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Star Wars: Episode III - Revenge of the Sith (2005) operates in the same action as Troy, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The tense payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Die Hard
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Die Hard (1988) belongs to the same conversation as Troy — both anchor their action in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Aliens
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Troy's action stayed with you, Aliens (1986) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The thriller through-line is the real common thread between them.

faq.question: What should I watch after Troy?
faq.answer: Try Kingdom of Heaven, 300, Spartacus—each shares DNA with Troy while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Troy?
faq.answer: Troy (2004) is best described as War, Action, History. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Troy worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.2/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this war lane.
